title: Independent Electricity Market Operator of the Philippines v. Energy Regulatory Commission
holding: Mandamus lies where an agency unreasonably delays or refuses to act on applications it is mandated by law to process, especially where inaction amounts to grave abuse or manifest injustice; the writ compels action, not a particular outcome.

links.relevance: PENRO's failure to act on the land-concession inquiry is compellable by mandamus once a duty to act exists.
Undue delay (>250 working days) is compellable; mandamus compels resolution.
MEO administrative inaction is compellable by mandamus.

title: Rio, Jr. v. Commission on Elections en Banc
holding: Reiterates the requisites of mandamus; the writ cannot compel a discretionary act in a particular way, but an agency has a duty to resolve pending matters expeditiously per its rules.

links.relevance: The agency's duty to resolve expeditiously grounds the §21 undue-delay/failure-to-render-service theory.
Duty to act on the application (issue OR / written disposition) expeditiously.

title: Province of Maguindanao del Norte v. Bureau of Local Government Finance
holding: Mandamus lies where the agency had a ministerial duty to process a matter once the legal requisites are met; courts will not control discretion but can compel performance of the ministerial step.

links.relevance: Compels the ministerial processing step once requisites are met.
Ministerial processing is compellable.

title: Philippine Statistics Authority v. Ferolino
holding: Where the agency's duty is ministerial (issuance of certified documents upon request and payment), mandamus properly compels performance; separate disputes must be litigated separately.

links.relevance: Failure to issue the OR / official document upon request is a ministerial default compellable by mandamus.
Ministerial issuance/records duty is compellable.

title: Esmero v. Duterte
holding: Mandamus compels performance of a pre-existing duty and does not lie to control discretion, particularly in areas committed to executive discretion.

links.relevance: The limit: frame the prayer to compel ACTION on the application, not a particular favorable outcome.
links.note: Guards the mandamus theory against a discretion objection.

links.relevance: Compel action, not a specific result.

title: DMCI Project Developers, Inc. v. Bernadas
holding: The Register of Deeds' duty to register an instrument that meets the requisites, and to implement a final and executory registrable order, is ministerial; an LRA 'registrable' finding is not itself registration/cancellation and the immutability of judgments applies.

links.relevance: The Registry of Deeds of Manila's non-compliance with the Feb-2019 court directive is a ministerial default; the final order must be implemented.
RD must implement a final registrable order affecting title.

title: Register of Deeds, Pasig, Rizal v. Heirs of Caiji
holding: The Register of Deeds, being a ministerial officer under the Torrens system, has no authority to pass upon the legality or regularity of a court order issued by a competent court; any challenge must be by ordinary action, not by RD refusal.

links.relevance: The RD cannot refuse to obey the annulment/reinstatement order by second-guessing it.

title: Angeles v. Secretary of Justice
holding: Mandamus will not issue if the applicant's right is in substantial doubt or the act is not purely ministerial; in land registration, where substantial issues (e.g., double-titling risk) exist, the RD/LRA cannot be compelled.

links.relevance: Anticipates the RD's defense that reinstatement raises title doubts; the answer is that pure implementation of a final order is ministerial.

title: Laburada v. Land Registration Authority
holding: Mandamus will not lie where the act is not purely ministerial due to doubt or dispute, such as whether the land is already covered by an existing Torrens title.

links.relevance: Frames why the reinstatement must proceed on the final judgment, not as a contested registrability question.

title: Poblete v. Banco Filipino Savings and Mortgage Bank
holding: A supplemental or clarificatory order may supply necessary consequences logically implied by a final judgment, but must not alter the substance of what has become final.

links.relevance: A supplemental judgment can implement (reinstate the title) but cannot replace the original decision — the exact issue in Capacuan.
links.note: Directly on the supplemental-vs-original judgment fight.

title: Dagondon v. Ladaga
holding: A final and executory judgment is immutable and unalterable regardless of alleged error; only narrow exceptions (void judgment, clerical error, supervening events) apply.

links.relevance: The Feb-2019 decision is immutable; execution must carry it out, not re-open it.

title: Ricafort v. Fajardo
holding: Supervening events after finality may justify the suspension or refusal of execution where enforcement would be unjust or inequitable.

links.relevance: Frames how post-judgment developments (RD inaction, intervening transfers) are handled at execution.

title: Manarin v. Manarin
holding: A petition to compel surrender of the owner's duplicate (Sec. 107, PD 1529) may be an incident of execution; the RTC may issue execution-related orders even against a non-party where intimately connected to execution and not altering the final judgment.

links.relevance: Mechanism to force surrender of the outstanding owner's duplicate so the RD can cancel/reissue per the judgment.
Compelling surrender of a withheld owner's duplicate to effect title recovery.
links.note: Applies to MWK title recovery too.
